title: SystemVerilog Interview Question Bank
description: Interview preparation covering SV language fundamentals, scheduling semantics, constrained randomization, OOP, and clocking blocks.

questions.prompt: Explain the difference between blocking and non-blocking assignments. When would you use each?
questions.rubric: Distinguish blocking (=) in Active region vs non-blocking (<=) deferred to NBA region. Must mention avoiding race conditions in always_ff.
questions.model_answer: Blocking (=) evaluates and updates immediately in the Active region. Non-blocking (<=) evaluates RHS immediately but defers the update to the NBA region. Use non-blocking in always_ff to avoid same-time-step races, and blocking in always_comb for combinational logic.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between a class and a module in SystemVerilog?
questions.rubric: Modules are static hardware hierarchy; classes are dynamic runtime objects with inheritance/polymorphism. Classes cannot have ports.
questions.model_answer: Modules are statically instantiated at elaboration, represent hardware, have ports, and can be synthesized. Classes are dynamic objects created with new(), support inheritance and polymorphism, and are used for testbenches. Modules cannot extend; classes can.

questions.prompt: Walk through the SV scheduling regions in a single time step. Where do assertions evaluate?
questions.rubric: Must list Active, NBA, Observed, Reactive, Postponed. Assertions sample in Observed after NBA. Program blocks run in Reactive.
questions.model_answer: Regions in order: Active (blocking, continuous assigns) → Inactive (#0) → NBA (non-blocking updates) → Observed (assertion sampling, clocking-block input) → Reactive (program blocks, assertion actions) → Postponed ($monitor). Assertions evaluate in Observed, seeing post-NBA settled values.

questions.prompt: A testbench uses @(posedge clk) to both drive and sample DUT signals. Why can this cause races?
questions.rubric: Both TB and RTL execute in Active region creating read/write races. Clocking blocks separate sampling (Observed) from driving (Re-Active).
questions.model_answer: @(posedge clk) triggers in Active alongside RTL, causing potential read-before-NBA-update races. Clocking blocks fix this: inputs sample in Observed (post-NBA, stable values), outputs drive in Re-Active (after assertions). This clean separation eliminates races.

questions.prompt: Are SystemVerilog constraints solved top-down or bidirectionally?
questions.rubric: Constraints are solved bidirectionally/simultaneously. A constraint on B can restrict A. Mention solve...before for ordering hints.
questions.model_answer: SV constraints are bidirectional — all constraints are gathered and solved simultaneously by a BDD/SAT solver. A constraint on B restricts A's domain even if A appears first. Use solve a before b to control marginal distribution ordering.

questions.prompt: Why do UVM testbenches use virtual interfaces?
questions.rubric: Interfaces are static; classes are dynamic. Virtual interface is a handle/pointer allowing class-based TB code to access static interface signals.
questions.model_answer: Interfaces are static module-like constructs instantiated at elaboration. Classes are dynamic. A virtual interface is a reference to an instantiated interface, allowing dynamic class-based components to access DUT signals. Passed via uvm_config_db.

questions.prompt: randomize() returns 0 and fields are all zero. Debug process?
questions.rubric: Check return value, use constraint_mode(0) to isolate, check inheritance conflicts, try rand_mode(0) per field, use simulator debug flags.
questions.model_answer: 1) Check return value. 2) Disable all constraints with constraint_mode(0) and re-randomize. 3) Re-enable one at a time to find conflicts. 4) Check parent-class constraint inheritance. 5) Use solve...before to isolate ordering. 6) Use simulator debug flags like -solvefaildebug.

questions.prompt: Explain fork-join, fork-join_any, and fork-join_none. When use each?
questions.rubric: join waits ALL, join_any waits ANY one, join_none continues immediately. Use cases: parallel stimulus, timeout racing, background monitors.
questions.model_answer: fork-join: waits for ALL children (parallel stimulus). fork-join_any: waits for ANY one child (timeout patterns). fork-join_none: parent continues immediately (background monitors). Always use disable fork to clean up orphaned processes.

questions.prompt: Design the verification methodology package hierarchy for a multi-project SoC platform with 5+ projects.
questions.rubric: Package layers (base → protocol → project), parameterized classes, constraint layering with inheritance, factory overrides, versioning.
questions.model_answer: Three layers: (1) Base layer: verif_base package with parameterized transactions, generic scoreboards, coverage utilities. (2) Protocol layer: per-protocol packages (axi_verif_pkg) with agents and checkers. (3) Project layer: project-specific environments composing protocol agents, project constraints via inheritance + constraint_mode. Use factory overrides for specialization. Maintain backward-compatible base APIs.

questions.prompt: Your regression is bottlenecked by constraint solving — each randomize() call takes 50ms. Root cause and fix?
questions.rubric: Must discuss constraint complexity (cross-field interactions), solver algorithm choice, constraint partitioning, pre/post_randomize, and architectural approaches like constraint reduction.
questions.model_answer: Root causes: (1) Large constraint sets with many cross-field interactions create exponential solver complexity. (2) Implication chains and soft constraints increase backtracking. Fixes: partition independent constraint groups into separate randomize calls, use solve...before to guide the solver, replace complex constraints with pre_randomize() procedural generation where possible, and consider constraint layering where base constraints are minimal and test-specific constraints are added. Profile with simulator constraint debug tools to identify the expensive constraint.

questions.prompt: Explain the difference between logic, wire, and reg. When must you use wire instead of logic?
questions.rubric: Must explain logic replaces both for single-driver signals (IEEE 1800-2023 §6.11.2), wire needed for multi-driver resolution, reg is legacy and misleading name.
questions.model_answer: logic can replace both reg and wire for any single-driver signal — it works in continuous assigns, procedural blocks, and module ports. wire is required when multiple drivers exist (tri-state bus, bidirectional pads) because it has a resolution function to handle conflicts. reg is a legacy name retained for backward compatibility — it does NOT imply a hardware register. Modern code should use logic everywhere except multi-driver nets.

questions.prompt: What happens when a 4-state value containing X is assigned to a 2-state bit variable? Why is this dangerous?
questions.rubric: Must explain X/Z silently become 0, masking uninitialized signals. Should mention $isunknown() and === as mitigations.
questions.model_answer: X and Z bits are silently converted to 0 when assigned to 2-state types like bit, int, or byte. This is dangerous because it masks uninitialized signals and reset bugs — the testbench sees 0 where the DUT has unknown state. Mitigations: use $isunknown() before the assignment to check for X/Z, keep DUT-facing signals as 4-state logic throughout the TB, and use === instead of == for X-aware comparisons.

questions.prompt: Explain packed vs. unpacked arrays. Give an example where using the wrong one causes a bug.
questions.rubric: Must distinguish contiguous bits (packed, sliceable) vs. independent elements (unpacked, not sliceable). Bug example should involve illegal part-select across unpacked boundaries.
questions.model_answer: Packed arrays store contiguous bits — logic [3:0][7:0] data is 32 bits that can be sliced as data[31:0]. Unpacked arrays store independent elements — logic [7:0] data [0:3] is 4 separate bytes where data[31:0] is illegal. Bug example: a serializer tries to stream all bytes as data[31:0] but the array is unpacked, causing a compile error or (worse with some tools) reading garbage from adjacent memory. Use packed for protocol bus lanes; unpacked for register files and memories.

questions.prompt: Compare dynamic arrays, queues, and associative arrays. When would you use each in a UVM testbench?
questions.rubric: Must accurately distinguish the structures: dynamic arrays (size known after construct, random access), queues (FIFO/LIFO, push/pop), and associative arrays (sparse key-value lookup).
questions.model_answer: Dynamic arrays (IEEE 1800-2023 §7.5): use when size is known after construction and random access is needed (e.g., pre-built stimulus sequences, coverage bins). Queues (§7.10): use for FIFO/LIFO ordering with efficient push/pop (e.g., driver stimulus queues, monitor output buffers). Bounded queues model hardware FIFO depth. Associative arrays (§7.8): use for sparse key-value lookup (e.g., scoreboard tracking outstanding TXNs by ID, config DB implementations). Always delete completed entries to avoid memory leaks.

questions.prompt: What is the performance difference between queue push_back vs. insert in the middle? Why does it matter in verification?
questions.rubric: Must identify push_back as O(1) and insert as O(N) due to array shifting. Should mention scoreboard bottlenecks.
questions.model_answer: push_back() and push_front() are O(1) — constant time regardless of queue size. insert(index, item) is O(N) because all subsequent elements must shift. In a scoreboard processing millions of transactions, mid-queue inserts can become a simulation bottleneck. If ordered insertion is needed, use an associative array with a sortable key.

questions.prompt: Your regression slows down over time and eventually runs out of memory. The scoreboard uses an associative array. What's the likely cause and fix?
questions.rubric: Must identify the lack of delete() calls causing unbounded memory growth. Should suggest checking .num() in report_phase.
questions.model_answer: The associative array is never cleaned up — matched or timed-out entries aren't being delete()'d. Over millions of transactions, the hash table grows unbounded, consuming memory and slowing lookups. Fix: Call expected_txns.delete(id) after every successful comparison. Add a report_phase() check that flags non-zero .num() as an error.

questions.prompt: How do array manipulation methods like find_index, sort, and sum work? Give a practical verification use case.
questions.rubric: Must show understanding of array locator and reduction methods. Provide concrete verification examples.
questions.model_answer: find_index with (item > threshold) returns indices matching a predicate; useful in coverage analysis to find which bins are uncovered. sort() / rsort() provide in-place ordering; useful to normalize transaction queues before comparison in an out-of-order scoreboard. sum() is a reduction method; useful to compute expected checksums from a packet payload array.
